g.data-narration: Understanding how light becomes pixels is fundamental to computer vision. This process enables machines to interpret the visual world accurately.
g.text: Understanding light-to-pixel conversion is essential for:

g.data-narration: The photometric pipeline shows how scene radiance travels through the camera system to become digital image values we can process.
Light sources come in various forms: point sources emit from a single point, directional sources like the sun come from infinity, area sources are extended, and ambient light is non-directional.
In diffuse or Lambertian reflection, light scatters equally in all directions. The surface brightness depends on the cosine of the angle between light and surface normal.
Specular reflection creates mirror-like highlights. Light reflects in a specific direction based on the surface normal, creating view-dependent bright spots.
This table summarizes key differences between diffuse and specular reflection, including direction, view dependence, and mathematical models.
The pinhole camera model is the simplest model where 3D points project through a tiny aperture. The x-coordinate equals focal length times X divided by Z.
Modern digital cameras convert light to pixels through a complex pipeline: lens focuses light, aperture controls amount, shutter sets duration, sensor captures photons, ADC converts to digital.
Radiometry measures physical light energy in watts. Photometry weights this by human eye sensitivity, measuring light as perceived in lumens.
The image irradiance equation relates scene radiance to sensor irradiance. Key factors include lens diameter, focal length, and the cosine fourth angle term.
The camera response function maps irradiance to pixel values through a nonlinear curve, typically including gamma correction and sensor saturation effects.
The Bayer pattern samples color using a grid of red, green, and blue filters: 50% green (matching eye sensitivity), 25% red, and 25% blue.
Photometric principles enable powerful computer vision applications: photometric stereo recovers surface normals, shape from shading estimates 3D shape from intensity.
g.text: Distinguish metals, plastics, fabric from BRDF
